title: Infantile Spasms (West Syndrome)
chief_complaint: My baby has these strange jerking movements where he suddenly bends forward, throws his arms out, and then cries. These happen in clusters of 10-20, especially when he wakes up. He's stopped smiling and rolling over - he's losing skills he had before.

physical_exam.Neurologic: Infantile spasms observed during exam: Flexor spasms (head drops forward, arms fling outward, legs flex) lasting 1-2 seconds each. Occur in clusters of 10-15. Irritable after cluster.
physical_exam.Development: Loss of previously acquired skills: No longer rolling over (did at 5 months). No social smile (had at 2 months). Poor head control. Hyperreflexia in all limbs.
physical_exam.Skin: Multiple hypopigmented macules (ash-leaf spots) visible under Wood lamp - Tuberous Sclerosis Complex stigmata.

hidden_data.eeg: Hypsarrhythmia: Chaotic, high-amplitude, disorganized background with multifocal spike and slow wave discharges. Ictal EEG during spasm: Sudden attenuation (electrodecremental response).
hidden_data.mri_brain: Multiple cortical tubers (frontal, temporal, parietal). Subependymal nodules along lateral ventricles. Giant cell astrocytoma at foramen of Monro (obstructive hydrocephalus).
hidden_data.genetic: TSC2 gene mutation (pathogenic) - confirms Tuberous Sclerosis Complex

correct_diagnosis: Infantile Spasms (West Syndrome) secondary to Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC2)
key_learning_points: Infantile spasms = severe epileptic encephalopathy of infancy. Peak onset 4-8 months. Triad: Epileptic spasms (flexor, extensor, or mixed) + Hypsarrhythmia on EEG + Developmental regression
Spasms often subtle - may be mistaken for colic, reflux, or startle. Key feature: OCCUR IN CLUSTERS upon awakening. Developmental regression is hallmark (loss of previously acquired skills)

Most common cause: Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (20%). Other causes: HIE, congenital infections, metabolic disorders, genetic (ARX, CDKL5). TSC diagnostic criteria: Skin (ash-leaf spots, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patch), Brain (cortical tubers, SENs, SEGA), Heart (rhabdomyomas), Kidneys (AMLs), Eyes (phakomas)

Treatment: FIRST-LINE: ACTH (adrenocorticotropic hormone) 150 units/m² IM daily ×2 weeks then taper OR Vigabatrin 50-150mg/kg/day (especially if TSC - GABA transaminase inhibitor). Response within 2 weeks predicts better prognosis

Prognosis: 70% have severe intellectual disability. Better if treated early (<1 month of onset) and normal MRI. 50% develop other seizure types (Lennox-Gastaut syndrome). TSC patients need lifelong surveillance for SEGA, AMLs, LAM
